content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
September 06, 2016 
Jolie Houston 
City Attorney, City of Los Altos 
Ten Almaden Blvd., 11th Floor 
San Jose, CA 95113-2233 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-16-095 
Dear Ms. Houston: 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Los Altos City Council member 
Mary Prochnow, regarding her duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the "Act").1 Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has 
already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political 
Practices Commission (the "Commission") docs not act as the finder of fact. Because you have not 
limited your questions to a specific governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for 
informal assistance.:! 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
Considering her multiple interests, including her real estate business and downtown 
property, may Councilmember Prochnow take part in various decisions involving the downtown 
area, general planning, zoning requirements, project approval requirements, and parking? 
FACTS 
You are the City Attorney for the City of Los Altos (the "City") and are requesting advice 
on behalf of Councilmember Mary Prochnow regarding her involvement in the various decisions 
identified below. The City is a general law city incorporated in December of 1952. It is located 3 7 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated. 
` lnfonnal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or fonnal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329( c)(3 ).) 


File No. J-16-095 
Page No. 2 
miles south and covers 7 square miles on the lower portion of the San Francisco Peninsula. It is 
bordered by Los Altos Hills, Palo Alto, Mountain View, Sunnyvale and Cupertino. 
The City has a population of approximately 29,500, with total number of households of 
10,810, and the total number of businesses of roughly 1,332. The City's downtown area is 
approximately one-tenth of a square mile and is commonly referred to as the "Downtown Triangle." 
The Downtown Triangle is served by ten City owned public Parking Plazas. Under the City's 
Zoning Code certain zoning districts (CRS, CRS/OAD, CD and CD/R3 Districts) can use the City's 
public Parking Plazas to meet the City's parking requirements. This is limited to the properties 
adjacent to the Parking Plazas and for those properties that participated in the City's Parking 
District. 
The City has a Downtown Design Plan and Design Guidelines. You state that these plans 
Are conceptual design plans adopted pursuant to the City's police powers. Pursuant to the City's 
police powers, the Downtown Design Plan or the Design Guidelines may be amended by a 
legislative action of the City Council, such as a resolution. 
As pertinent to the decisions you have identified, you have stated that Councilmember 
Prochnow is also a real estate rcaltor and broker working in the City. Councilmember Prochnow 
operates her business (Prochnow Realtors, Inc.) from property she co-owns within the City's 
downtown area. She also has a residential property more than 500 feet from the downtown area. 
Councilmember Prochnow has an eighty percent (80%) ownership interest in Prochnow 
Realtors, Inc. and a fifty percent (50%) ownership interest in the building and property in the 
downtown area. In addition to commission income received as a real tor and broker, Councilmcmber 
Prochnow receives rental income from 13 tenants who rent space within the downtown building. 
In regard to the downtown building, you state: 
• The majority of the leases are month-to-month. 
• There are no leases over three years. 
• Tenants will likely use the City public parking located in Plaza 6. 
• There are 10 City Parking Plazas (numbered 1-10) that are part of the City's Parking 
District, which her tenants may also use. 
ANALYSIS AND CONCLUSIONS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.) A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. Different standards 
apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material 
depending on the nature of the interest. 


File No. 1-16-095 
Page No. 3 
Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103. Those 
interests implicated by your account of the facts are the following: 
• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)). 
• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of S2,000 or 
more (Section 87103(b)) including a pro rata share of interests in real property of any 
business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, 
indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater (Section 82033 ). 
• An interest in a source of income to the official, or promised income, which aggregates to 
$500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c)) including any 
community property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro rata share of the income of 
any business entity or trust in which the official (or his or her spouse) owns directly, 
indirectly, or beneficially, a I 0-percent or greater interest (Section 82030(a)). 
• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.) 
Prochnow Realtors/Rental Business: Councilmember Prochnow has interests in Prochnow 
Realtors and her Rental Business as business entities. Assuming Councilmember Prochnow's pro 
rata share of income from Prochnow Realtors and her Rental Business is $500 or more in the 12 
months prior to the decision, she also has an interests in the businesses as a sources of income. 
(Section 87103(a), (c) and (d).) 
Clients of Prochnow Realtors: Councilmember Prochnow has an interest in any client of 
Prochnow Realtors if the councilmember's pro rata share of income derived from the customer 
aggregates to $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the governmental decision. (Section 
87103(c).) 
Real Property: Councilmember Prochnow has a real property interest in the Main Street 
Property and her residence. (Section 87103(b).) 
Tenants: Councilmember Prochnow has an interest in any tenant of the Main Street 
Property as a source of income if her pro rata share of income from any tenant aggregates to $500 
or more in the 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) 
You have identified numerous categories of decisions that the city council may be faced 
with in relationship to the downtown area. We address these decisions generally below after 
categorizing the various decisions. 3 
l For easier reference, mdividual decisions are numbered below as originally numbered m your request for 
assistance. 


File No. I-16-095 
Page No. 4 
Decision Group One (Minor Zoning Decisions Applicable to all Properties within 
Zoning Category): 
• 
I. An amendment to the Zoning Code Section 14.74 for new parking ratio requirements that 
will be applied City-wide for all office and commercial zoning districts. 
• 3. An amendment to the Zoning Code Section 14.74 for new development standards for off­
street parking that will be applied City-wide for all of the City's office and commercial 
zoning districts. 
• 8. An amendment to the Zoning Code to limit the height, increase setback requirements and 
require wider sidewalks in the CD and CD /R3 Districts. 
• 9. An amendment to the Zoning Code to modify the Height Limitation Exceptions and 
Definitions to only apply to flagpoles, radio and television antennas and transmission 
towers. This amendment would require all other architectural features to have screening and 
will be applied City-wide for all office and commercial zoning districts. 
• 10. An amendment to the Zoning Code to establish a maximum height for all rooftop 
architectural features that are used for aesthetic or screening purposes. This amendment will 
be applied City-wide for all office and commercial zoning districts. 
• 15. An amendment to the Zoning Code or Downtown Design Guidelines to preserve views 
of the surrounding hills and open space. This amendment will be applied City-wide for all 
commercial, office and multi-family residential zoning districts. This does not require 
General or Specific Plan amendments. 
• 16. Adoption of new Zoning Code provisions to modify the City's planning process that will 
be applied City-wide and require all building design review in the City's commercial, office 
and multi-family residential zoning districts to include a review by 2 architects and a 
landscape architect prior to coming to the Planning and Transportation Commission and 
ultimately to the City Council. This does not require General or Specific Plan amendments. 
Regulation l 8702.2(a)(2) states that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial interest is 
material whenever the governmental decision "[d]etermines the parcel's zoning or rezoning (other 
than a zoning decision applicable to all properties designated in that category) .... " For zoning 
decisions of a similar nature, we have previously advised you that "[s]o long as the zoning decision 
is applicable to all commercial or office buildings, we find no reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on any of Councilmember Prochnow's interests." (Houston Advice Letter, No. A-
14-207.) Similarly, for each of the decisions described in this group that amend a zoning code 


File No. l-16-095 
Page No. 5 
ordinance as applicable for all properties within the zoning district, we find no reasonably 
foreseeable material effect on any of Councilmember Prochnow's interests.4 
Decision Group Two (Interest Explicitly Involved in Decisions) 
• 2. An amendment to the Zoning Code Section 14. 74 for new parking ratio requirements for 
properties within the City's Parking District. 
• 5. Formation of a new Parking District that includes all of the existing Parking Plazas. 
• 6. Formation of a new Parking District that expands access to the existing Parking Plazas to 
other properties outside of the existing Parking District. 
• 7. Adoption of a parking in-lieu fee ordinance that will be applied to all projects in the 
City's Downtown Triangle. 
• 18. Participating in a "visioning" process that may lead to the adoption of a Downtown 
Master Plan. 
• 14. Creating a process or policy for including light/shadow modelling for proposed 
development. This process or policy will be applied for all commercial, office and multi­
family residential projects located in the Downtown Triangle. This does not require Zoning 
Code, General or Specific Plan amendments. 
• 
19. An amendment to the City's preferred parking pennit program to increase the permit 
fees and/or increase the number of permits available. This program is currently being used 
in the City's Parking Plazas and is only available to properties within the Parking District. 
Pursuant to Regulations 18702.l(a) and 18702.2(a)(l)-(6), an interest in real property is 
explicitly involved in the decision and the effect of the decision on the interest is both foreseeable 
and material if the decision: 
"( 1) Involves the adoption or amendment to a general plan ... or specific 
plan, and the parcel is located within the proposed boundaries of the plan; 
"(2) Determines the parcel's zoning or rezoning ... , annexation or de­
annexation, or inclusion in or exclusion from any city, county, district, or other local 
government subdivision, or other boundaries ... ; 
"(3) Would impose, repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that 
apply to the parcel; 
4 We also note that the minor zoning decisions you have identified are not of the magnitude or nature that a 
reasonably foreseeable and material effect on your interestO is likely, under Regulations 18702.l(b) and 18702.2(a)(7)­
( 12), as applicable to interests not-explicitly involved in the decisions. 


File No. 1-16-095 
Page No. 6 
"(5) Involves the issuance, denial or revocation of a license, pennit or other 
land usc entitlement authorizing a specific use of or improvement to the parcel or 
any variance that changes the pennitted use of, or restrictions placed on, that real 
property." (Regulation 18702.2(a).) 
Applied to the decisions identified in this group: 
• Amending a zoning code as applicable only to properties within the Parking District and the 
fonnation of a new Parking District are decisions falling under Regulation 18702.2(a)(2). 
• The adoption of a parking in-lieu fee and amending the parking pennit program to increase 
pennit fees are decisions falling under Regulation I 8702.2(a)(3 ). 
• Requiring light/shadow modelling or other development criteria applicable only to the 
Downtown Triangle is a decision involving a land use entitlement authorizing a specific use 
of or improvement to the parcel or any variance that changes the pennitted use of, or 
restrictions placed on, that real property" falling under Regulation 18702.2(a)(5). 
• 
Participating in a "visioning" process for the downtown area that may lead to a Downtown 
Master Plan is a decision that falls under Regulation 18702.2(a)( 1) or (5). 5 
Accordingly, Councilmember Prochnow's interest in the Main Street Property is explicitly 
involved in all of the decisions within this brroup and the effect of the decision is both foreseeable 
and material. Councilmember Prochnow may not make, participate, or use her position to influence 
these decisions. 
Decision Group Three (Interest Non-Explicitly Involved in Decision) 
• 12. An amendment to the City's planning submittal requirements for all commercial, office 
and multi-family residential design review. This does not require Zoning Code, General or 
Specific Plan amendments. 
• 13. An amendment to the City's planning submittal requirements to be applied City-wide for 
all commercial, office and multi-family residential projects to improve the landscaping 
requirements. This does not require Zoning Code, General or Specific Plan amendments. 
s Whtie you have not identified the "Downtown Master Plan" as a "Specific" plan, as stated by the Governor's 
Office of Planning and Research, "Practicing planners in California have used a variety of euphemisms to describe 
specific plans. The creative use and combination of planning tenns to describe various planning tools has blurred the 
distinction between specific plans, community plans, and area plans to the extent that the tenns are often misused." 
(The Planner's Guide to Specific Plans (2001) available at https:l1www.opr.ca.g0\' docs.'specific plans.pd[) To the 
extent that a "master" plan or "design" plan is indeed a "specific" plan or involves the amendment of the General Plan, 
a decision involving the plan falls within Regulation l 8702.2(a)( I). However, even assuming that a plan is not a 
··specific" plan and does not involve the amendment of the General Plan, any decision invoh·ing a plan that establishes 
development criteria which will apply to the property interest in question is a decision involving "a land use entitlement 
authorizing a specific use of or improvement to the parcel or any variance that changes the pennitted use of. or 
restrictions placed on, that real property" and the property interest ,s explicitly involved in the decision pursuant to 
Regulations 18702.l(a) and 18702.2(a)(5). 


File No. I-1 6-095 
Page No. 7 
If a financial interest is not a named party in, or subject of the decision, the interest in not­
explicitly involved in the decision and the effect of the decision is reasonably foreseeable if the 
effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
(Regulation 18701.) In detennining if a reasonable foreseeable effect is material, different 
materiality standards apply depending on the interest affected by the decision. (Regulation 18702.) 
Based upon the facts provided, it appears that Councilmember Prochnow's interests are not­
explicitly involved in decisions regarding planning submittal requirements. However, we are unable 
to make a detennination as to whether these decisions will have a reasonable foreseeable material 
effect on her interests without a more complete description of the proposed requirements. The 
magnitude and nature of the specific requirements imposed must be considered in detennining 
whether a financial effect is foreseeable and material. 
Decision Group Four (No or Nominal Effect on Interest) 
• 4. Adoption of a City parking policy that will re-stripe some andtor all of the City Parking 
Plazas to increase the available parking that \Viii accommodate and/or facilitate the 
development of adjacent parcels. 
• 11. An amendment to the City's Downtown Design Plan and Design Guidelines to add 
better examples, including updated photographs, of the desirable looks and articulations for 
buildings in the downtown. This will require a Downtown Design Plan and Design 
Guidelines amendments, but not Zoning Code, General or Specific Plan amendments. 
• 17. Re-naming the Downtown Design Guidelines to Design Requirements, include more 
detailed planning process check lists and revising the City's Website planning page to 
include all steps in the planning process. This does not involve Zoning Code, General or 
Specific Plan amendments. 
Under Regulations 18702(b), a financial effect is not material ifit is nominal, 
inconsequential, or insignificant. In regards to re-striping the existing parking plaza adjacent to her 
Main Street Property, we previously advised Councilmember Prochnow that ''it is unlikely that the 
mere re-striping of exiting parking plazas will have any financial effect on her interests." (Houston 
Advice Letter, No. A-14-207.) Similarly, it is unlikely that the re-striping of the other existing 
parking plaza's will have any financial effect on her various interests. 
In regards to amending the City's Downtown Design Plan and Design Guidelines to "add 
better examples," "include more detailed check lists," and "re-name," we find that non-substantive 
clarifications and re-naming would not result in a significant financial effect. Provided that the 
clarifying changes do not add substantive requirements, Councilmember Prochnow is not prohibited 
from taking part in these decisions. 
Decision Group Five (No Foreseeable Effect on Interest Pending Study) 
• 20. Adoption of a "smart meter" (parking meters) program for the City. This will include a 
request for proposals for a smart meter consultant to conduct a comprehensive study of the 
City's zoning districts where the smart meters will be installed. 


File No. 1-16-095 
Page No. 8 
Previously, we have advised Councilmember Prochnow "that any effects of a decision to 
commission a parking study are purely hypothetical prior to the start of the study and that 
identifiable effects will be contingent on future decisions implementing the results of the study." 
Accordingly, we found that the effects of a decision to commission a parking study were not 
reasonably foreseeable. (Houston Advice Letter, No. A-14-207; see also Houston Advice Letter No. 
A-15-183.) Similarly, Councilmember Prochnow is not prohibited from taking part in the decisions 
regarding a request for proposals for a smart meter consultant to conduct a comprehensive study. 
However, we are unable to reach any conclusions regarding Councilmember Prochnow's 
involvement in implementing the program after the completion of the study as her further 
participation will hinge on the results of the study. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
BGL:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Brian G. Lau 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

Considering her multiple interests, including her real estate business and downtown 
property, may Councilmember Prochnow take part in various decisions involving the downtown 
area, general planning, zoning requirements, project approval requirements, and parking?

and material. Councilmember Prochnow may not make, participate, or use her position to influence 
these decisions. 
Decision Group Three (Interest Non-Explicitly Involved in Decision) 
• 12. An amendment to the City's planning submittal requirements for all commercial, office 
and multi-family residential design review. This does not require Zoning Code, General or 
Specific Plan amendments. 
• 13. An amendment to the City's planning submittal requirements to be applied City-wide for 
all commercial, office and multi-family residential projects to improve the landscaping 
requirements. This does not require Zoning Code, General or Specific Plan amendments. 
s Whtie you have not identified the "Downtown Master Plan" as a "Specific" plan, as stated by the Governor's 
Office of Planning and Research, "Practicing planners in California have used a variety of euphemisms to describe 
specific plans. The creative use and combination of planning tenns to describe various planning tools has blurred the 
distinction between specific plans, community plans, and area plans to the extent that the tenns are often misused." 
(The Planner's Guide to Specific Plans (2001) available at https:l1www.opr.ca.g0\' docs.'specific plans.pd[) To the 
extent that a "master" plan or "design" plan is indeed a "specific" plan or involves the amendment of the General Plan, 
a decision involving the plan falls within Regulation l 8702.2(a)( I). However, even assuming that a plan is not a 
··specific" plan and does not involve the amendment of the General Plan, any decision invoh·ing a plan that establishes 
development criteria which will apply to the property interest in question is a decision involving "a land use entitlement 
authorizing a specific use of or improvement to the parcel or any variance that changes the pennitted use of. or 
restrictions placed on, that real property" and the property interest ,s explicitly involved in the decision pursuant to 
Regulations 18702.l(a) and 18702.2(a)(5). 


File No. I-1 6-095  
If a financial interest is not a named party in, or subject of the decision, the interest in not­
explicitly involved in the decision and the effect of the decision is reasonably foreseeable if the 
effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
(Regulation 18701.) In detennining if a reasonable foreseeable effect is material, different 
materiality standards apply depending on the interest affected by the decision. (Regulation 18702.) 
Based upon the facts provided, it appears that Councilmember Prochnow's interests are not­
explicitly involved in decisions regarding planning submittal requirements. However, we are unable 
to make a detennination as to whether these decisions will have a reasonable foreseeable material 
effect on her interests without a more complete description of the proposed requirements. The 
magnitude and nature of the specific requirements imposed must be considered in detennining 
whether a financial effect is foreseeable and material. 
Decision Group Four (No or Nominal Effect on Interest) 
• 4. Adoption of a City parking policy that will re-stripe some andtor all of the City Parking 
Plazas to increase the available parking that \Viii accommodate and/or facilitate the 
development of adjacent parcels. 
• 11. An amendment to the City's Downtown Design Plan and Design Guidelines to add 
better examples, including updated photographs, of the desirable looks and articulations for 
buildings in the downtown. This will require a Downtown Design Plan and Design 
Guidelines amendments, but not Zoning Code, General or Specific Plan amendments. 
• 17. Re-naming the Downtown Design Guidelines to Design Requirements, include more 
detailed planning process check lists and revising the City's Website planning page to 
include all steps in the planning process. This does not involve Zoning Code, General or 
Specific Plan amendments. 
Under Regulations 18702(b), a financial effect is not material ifit is nominal, 
inconsequential, or insignificant. In regards to re-striping the existing parking plaza adjacent to her 
Main Street Property, we previously advised Councilmember Prochnow that ''it is unlikely that the 
mere re-striping of exiting parking plazas will have any financial effect on her interests." (Houston 
Advice Letter, No. A-14-207.) Similarly, it is unlikely that the re-striping of the other existing 
parking plaza's will have any financial effect on her various interests. 
In regards to amending the City's Downtown Design Plan and Design Guidelines to "add 
better examples," "include more detailed check lists," and "re-name," we find that non-substantive 
clarifications and re-naming would not result in a significant financial effect. Provided that the 
clarifying changes do not add substantive requirements, Councilmember Prochnow is not prohibited 
from taking part in these decisions. 
Decision Group Five (No Foreseeable Effect on Interest Pending Study) 
• 20. Adoption of a "smart meter" (parking meters) program for the City. This will include a 
request for proposals for a smart meter consultant to conduct a comprehensive study of the 
City's zoning districts where the smart meters will be installed.  
Previously, we have advised Councilmember Prochnow "that any effects of a decision to 
commission a parking study are purely hypothetical prior to the start of the study and that 
identifiable effects will be contingent on future decisions implementing the results of the study." 
Accordingly, we found that the effects of a decision to commission a parking study were not 
reasonably foreseeable. (Houston Advice Letter, No. A-14-207; see also Houston Advice Letter No. 
A-15-183.) Similarly, Councilmember Prochnow is not prohibited from taking part in the decisions 
regarding a request for proposals for a smart meter consultant to conduct a comprehensive study. 
However, we are unable to reach any conclusions regarding Councilmember Prochnow's 
involvement in implementing the program after the completion of the study as her further 
participation will hinge on the results of the study.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Considering her multiple interests, including her real estate business and downtown 
property, may Councilmember Prochnow take part in various decisions involving the downtown 
area, general planning, zoning requirements, project approval requirements, and parking?
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION • 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 September 06, 2016 Jolie Houston City Attorney, City of Los Altos Ten Almaden Blvd., 11th Floor San Jose, CA 95113-2233 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. 1-16-095 Dear Ms. Houston: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Los Altos City Council member Mary Prochnow, regarding her duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") docs not act as the finder of fact. Because you have not limited your questions to a specific governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.:! Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. QUESTION Considering her multiple interests, including her real estate business and downtown property, may Councilmember Prochnow take part in various decisions involving the downtown area, general planning, zoning requirements, project approval requirements, and parking? FACTS You are the City Attorney for the City of Los Altos (the "City") and are requesting advice on behalf of Councilmember Mary Prochnow regarding her involvement in the various decisions identified below. The City is a general law city incorporated in December of 1952. It is located 3 7 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated. ` lnfonnal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or fonnal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329( c)(3 ).) File No. J-16-095 Page No. 2 miles south and covers 7 square miles on the lower portion of the San Francisco Peninsula. It is bordered by Los Altos Hills, Palo Alto, Mountain View, Sunnyvale and Cupertino. The City has a population of approximately 29,500, with total number of households of 10,810, and the total number of businesses of roughly 1,332. The City's downtown area is approximately one-tenth of a square mile and is commonly referred to as the "Downtown Triangle." The Downtown Triangle is served by ten City owned public Parking Plazas. Under the City's Zoning Code certain zoning districts (CRS, CRS/OAD, CD and CD/R3 Districts) can use the City's public Parking Plazas to meet the City's parking requirements. This is limited to the properties adjacent to the Parking Plazas and for those properties
